title: Rootless overlay-on-btrfs: post-reboot state refresh spends ~30-90s in per-file unlinkat() removing a --rm container's layer, blocking the first podman command
body:
### Issue Description

On a rootless setup with the `overlay` graph driver on a **btrfs** backing filesystem, the **first** podman command after an unclean shutdown (or any reboot with an autoremove container that was still running) blocks for tens of seconds. libpod runs its one-time per-boot state refresh on that first command (missing `runtimeAliveFile`, cf. #13240). The refresh removes containers created with `--rm`, and that removal deletes the container's writable layer **file by file, synchronously, inside the first command** - a plain `podman ps` does not return until it finishes.

strace of the refresh shows the cost is almost entirely `unlinkat()`:

```
% time     seconds  usecs/call     calls    errors syscall
------ ----------- ----------- --------- --------- ----------------
 98.01   34.532826         416     82911     16604 unlinkat
  1.99    0.700678          42     16620         1 openat
  0.00    0.000906         906         1           unlink
  0.00    0.000243          18        13         6 newfstatat
------ ----------- ----------- --------- --------- ----------------
100.00   35.234694         353     99547     16611 total
```

A second, independent capture of the same scenario shows the same shape (98.6% unlinkat, 57985 calls, 28.9s), so the profile is stable across runs.

~83k `unlinkat()` calls at ~0.4 ms each on btrfs = ~35s of kernel time (45s wall) to delete one container's rw layer (a kernel source tree unpacked into the container as the test payload). Wall-clock time scales with rw-layer content: an empty alpine is instant, the kernel-tree layer takes ~30-45s, and the original field incident (a development container after a work session) took ~89s. The failing unlinkat calls are not waste from a duplicated pass: their count matches the openat count almost exactly (16604 vs 16620 here; 9445 vs 9474 in the second capture), which is the signature of a plain recursive remove (Go os.RemoveAll style): one openat per directory to list it, one unlinkat that fails with EISDIR, then unlinkat(AT_REMOVEDIR). So the cost is simply N_files + 2*N_dirs sequential unlink/rmdir syscalls, each ~0.4 ms on btrfs, executed synchronously inside the refresh.

This is the same btrfs removal slowness as #26975 ("Cleanup after podman build takes forever with btrfs driver"), reached via a different path: here it runs inside the per-boot state refresh and therefore serializes the first podman command of the session - which on desktop systems is often issued by tooling (GNOME's Ptyxis terminal runs `podman ps` at startup; the ~89s stall made the terminal fail to launch, see the linked Ptyxis issue).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Reproducible without a reboot by simulating one (kill the container's processes so no cleanup runs, then clear the volatile state libpod uses for reboot detection):

```
# 1. an autoremove container with a large rw layer (~58k files):
git -C ~/linux archive --format=tar HEAD > /tmp/linux.tar
podman run -d --rm --name bigrw -v /tmp/linux.tar:/src.tar:ro,Z \
  docker.io/library/alpine sh -c 'mkdir /src && tar xf /src.tar -C /src && touch /READY && sleep infinity'
# wait until /READY exists: podman exec bigrw ls /READY

# 2. simulate the unclean reboot:
kill -9 $(podman inspect -f '{{.State.ConmonPid}}' bigrw) $(podman inspect -f '{{.State.Pid}}' bigrw)
rm -f "$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR"/libpod/tmp/alive* /dev/shm/libpod_rootless_lock_1000

# 3. time the first podman command:
/usr/bin/time -v podman --log-level=debug ps
```

A real hard reset (`echo b > /proc/sysrq-trigger`) with the container running reproduces the same thing on actual boot.

To capture the syscall profile on rootless (strace cannot spawn podman directly because ptrace blocks the setuid newuidmap): attach to the re-exec'd child by PPID within the first seconds:

```
strace -f -c -e trace=%file -o refresh.strace -p $(pgrep -P $(pgrep -nx podman))
```

### Describe the results you received

`podman ps` blocked for ~21-45s in different runs (~89s in the original incident), with system time dominating:

```
Elapsed (wall clock) time: 0:21.55     (smaller rw layer run)
User time (seconds): 0.12
System time (seconds): 19.52
File system inputs: 149192
```

Debug log of the refresh (note the gap between the cleanup lines and completion; metacopy is probed once and cleanly disabled, it is unrelated):

```
backingFs=btrfs, projectQuotaSupported=false, useNativeDiff=false, usingMetacopy=false
12:23:08  Podman detected system restart - performing state refresh
12:23:08  Removing container 9ec1b90d...
12:23:08  Cleaning up container 9ec1b90d...
[empty ps output]
12:23:53  Called ps.PersistentPostRunE     <-- ~45s later
```

strace summary of that window: see above (98.6% unlinkat).

### Describe the results you expected

Removing an autoremove container's writable layer should not serialize the first podman command after boot for tens of seconds. Options that would fix or mitigate:

- rename the layer directory into a staging/garbage area atomically and delete
it in the background or lazily (deferred removal), instead of a synchronous recursive per-file unlink inside the refresh;
- or run the refresh's container removal asynchronously so that read-only
commands like `podman ps` do not wait for it;
- independently, the ~1/6 failing unlinkat calls suggest a duplicated removal
pass worth eliminating.

Even granting that per-file unlink on btrfs is inherently expensive (#26975), paying that cost synchronously inside the first `podman ps` of the session is the part that hurts.

### podman info output

Key fields (full output attached):

```
graphDriverName: overlay
graphStatus.Backing Filesystem: btrfs
graphStatus.Native Overlay Diff: "false"
graphStatus.Using metacopy: "false"
databaseBackend: sqlite
version: 6.0.0 (Fedora, commit a8ed4b6dd12992decf659cadfdfb3d0cb1937748)
crun 1.28, conmon 2.2.1, kernel 7.2.0-rc1 (locally built)
distribution: fedora 45 (rawhide), rootless
```

full `podman info` here
```

  cpuUtilization:

  cpus: 32

  distribution:

  freeLocks: 2048
  hostname: primary-ws.local
  idMappings:

    - container_id: 0
      host_id: 1000

    uidmap:

  kernel: 7.2.0-rc1-4a50a141f05a-with-fixes-v1+
  linkmode: dynamic

  OsArch: linux/amd64
  Version: 6.0.0
```

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Fedora package podman-6.0.0 (Rawhide). Not retested against upstream HEAD.

### Additional environment details

Rootless, overlay driver, graphroot `~/.local/share/containers/storage` on btrfs. No user storage.conf. `metacopy=on` seen in graphOptions comes from the built-in default, is rejected under rootless `userxattr` and cleanly disabled; verified unrelated to the latency.

### Additional information

Full debug log and raw strace summary attached.
- [podman-refresh4.log](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29625996/podman-refresh4.log)
- [podman-refresh4.strace.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/29626000/podman-refresh4.strace.txt)

Related:
- #26975 (btrfs cleanup disproportionately slow; same underlying cost)
- #13240 (reboot detection via missing runtimeAliveFile)

Context / provenance: found while debugging why GNOME's Ptyxis terminal failed to launch after a power loss (its agent runs `podman ps` synchronously at startup): https://gitlab.gnome.org/chergert/ptyxis/-/issues/589

comments.body:
> Removing an autoremove container's writable layer should not serialize the first podman command after boot for tens of seconds. Options that would fix or mitigate:
> 
>     * rename the layer directory into a staging/garbage area atomically and delete
>       it in the background or lazily (deferred removal), instead of a synchronous recursive per-file unlink inside the refresh;
> 


Well our deletion code should already doing this but we cannot just lie. If a caller requests removal they expect removal. We are not a daemon process where we can schedule this for later. With our current staging logic the next podman command will always attempt to continue the cleanup in case the prior command crashed so not doing this would just make the next command hang longer which is not any better. 


>     * or run the refresh's container removal asynchronously so that read-only
>       commands like `podman ps` do not wait for it;

podman ps not fully read only though, a refresh by design has to rewrite the entire state. If there was a container with --rm people expect that container to be deleted by the time podman ps runs, asynchronously means we either still show a container that should have been deleted already or we lie and omit it but then we do not reflect the truth and someone might wonder where all the disk space is...
And then asynchronously where, again there is daemon process? We could have something fork in the bakground but that makes it much worse as there is no way for a user to know something is still happening.

> 
>     * independently, the ~1/6 failing unlinkat calls suggest a duplicated removal
>       pass worth eliminating.
> 

that is something that could be looked into, I am not sure what errors you are getting but just from the go stb removal calls it seems to try unlinkat first to remove a file, but if that path was a directory then it will have to cleanup it recursively before calling unlinkat again with AT_REMOVEDIR so for every directory I would expect one failed unlinkat if I read the code right.

You read the code right — the numbers confirm it exactly. Across two independent captures the failed unlinkat count matches the openat count almost 1:1 (16604 failed unlinkat vs 16620 openat in one run, 9445 vs 9474 in the other), i.e. one openat per directory to list it, one unlinkat that returns EISDIR, then unlinkat(AT_REMOVEDIR). So there is no duplicated removal pass — my wording in the report was wrong; the failures are exactly the expected per-directory EISDIR from the stdlib removal path. The cost is simply N_files + 2*N_dirs sequential unlinkat/rmdir at ~0.4 ms each on btrfs, run synchronously in the refresh.

Understood on the daemonless constraint, and fair points that staging/deferral only moves the same cost onto the next command and that `ps` must reflect true state after a refresh.

One clarifying question on where the cost is bound, though. The refresh bundles two things: (1) making the DB consistent — marking the ex-running container dead, and a --rm container as to-be-removed — which is cheap, and (2) physically deleting the layer (~66k unlinkat), which is expensive. Is the "next command continues the cleanup" continuation currently triggered on *every* command, or only on ones that touch storage?

Could the refresh do only (1) synchronously — so read-only queries return immediately and already no longer show the --rm container — and defer (2) to the next command that actually needs storage (run/pull/build/rm) plus `system prune`, rather than to whichever command runs first? Read-only queries derive "not running" from live processes anyway and don't need the layer gone. The tradeoff: if the user only ever runs read-only commands the dead layer's disk stays used until a storage-touching command runs; `podman system df` could report it as pending-removal so the space isn't a mystery.

The reason this matters in practice: the command that paid the ~90s in my case was a pure reader. The caller (GNOME Ptyxis' agent) issues exactly one podman command at startup — `podman ps --all --format=json` — which I verified by wrapping podman in PATH and logging argv: a single read-only enumeration, no run/start/pull. It just happened to be the first podman command after an unclean boot, so it inherited the full refresh + layer deletion.

I can see some argument to moving the removal to a goroutine that doesn't block the completion of state refresh (basically remove the entries from the database first, then do the expensive storage removal in a separate thread that runs parallel to the main command we intend to execute), but that can leave us in an awkward state where now your command does what it's supposed to, then hangs for 30 seconds after printing output while waiting for the removal to finish? And what happens if someone kills Podman in that time (which I would very much expect if we're sitting there, seemingly doing nothing, long after the command seemingly finished successfully)? We're left with a storage leak due to incomplete removal.

I suppose we could fork off background processes to handle the cleanup - `podman container cleanup` already runs in that way? - but that brings its own complications (how do we force removal of the storage when the container is already removed from the database? Do we need a `podman rm --storage` equivalent?)

> I suppose we could fork off background processes to handle the cleanup - podman container cleanup already runs in that way? - but that brings its own complications (how do we force removal of the storage when the container is already removed from the database? Do we need a podman rm --storage equivalent?)

Well the main issue with forking in the background is that no process manager will expect us to do that. When you use podman as part of systemd it will start killing all processes in that cgroup once the main pid exits and that is the container, so any oneshot quadlet/container will cause this break and just kill the forked off process.
To work around this you would need to add custom cgroup moving code, like we have with the pause process, but this gets ugly when nobody expects such background worker process to exists. We already have people complaining about the pause process in certain settings.

> And what happens if someone kills Podman in that time (which I would very much expect if we're sitting there, seemingly doing nothing, long after the command seemingly finished successfully)? We're left with a storage leak due to incomplete removal.

So the way it works today in storage is you can delete a layer and that will rename the directory with the layer content to a temp dir and then start deleting the files. If that process is killed any new command which inits the storage again (so basically every command, even podman ps) will pick up the work and continue deleting contents.

So in short this problem is not even limited to the first podman command, any command could start blocking for a while deleting files if another command was killed during layer removal.

Also note that podman ps today can block for many other reasons as well, today it takes the container lock and if you start a container with a volume that contains many files and which need selinux relabelling than ps blocks until it can get the lock once the container is started. On my server I have big volumes that take up to 5 minutes to relabel so a podman ps blocks for the full time today.

On that front I think we have more room for improvements which I plan to look into at some point to not make "read only"  commands take container locks where possible.

Thanks, that clears it up — I didn't realize the removal is already staged (rename to temp + unlink) and that any storage-initializing command resumes it, so this isn't really "first command after boot" but "whoever resumes an interrupted removal." And the systemd cgroup point against a background worker makes sense; a killed fork leaking half-removed storage is clearly worse.

The read-only-commands-not-taking-container-locks direction you mentioned sounds like the more general win here — the `podman ps` that triggered my case is a pure reader (verified: it issues only `podman ps --all --format=json`), and your 5-minute-relabel example is the same class: a reader blocked on work it doesn't need. Happy to test anything on that front.

Independently of when/where removal runs: the traversal is sequential and each unlinkat is btrfs metadata work (~0.4 ms). A bounded-parallel removal in containers/storage would cut wall time several-fold whoever ends up resuming it, and would help #26975 too. Is that something you'd take a patch/testing for?
